I've had an issue when using [`io.BytesIO` objects](https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#binary-i-o) with Python 2.7's version of `gzip.open`.
It turns out `gzip.open` only gained support for *filename* being a file object in [Python 3.3+](https://docs.python.org/3/library/gzip.html#gzip.open).

I see two options to work-around this limitation:
- skip the tests on Python 2
- make the tests work with real files in a temporary directory

For the time being, I've chosen the first option, because I like the speed and simplicity of using `io.BytesIO` objects.

I dug into this a little more and did a bunch of timings. The takeaway (consistent with earlier results from @jamesls and @mweiden) is that 128 MB lambdas are pretty jittery in terms of their coldstart performance, while 256 MB and above are not. The smaller (128MB) containers stall their startup at a rate of 2-3%, resulting in API Gateway timeouts. Hotstarts never have this problem, and 256MB+ lambdas never seem to have it either.

Compiling Python bytecode cache may help a little bit when importing a lot of code (tens of megabytes) but the signal is not strong. It might become stronger if we were to put more libraries into the package.

## Test scenario
This scenario is intended to reflect our app's imports and payload.

### Setup
`chalice new-project`
#### app.py
```
import os
from chalice import Chalice

app = Chalice(app_name='timings')

import connexion
import boto3
import elasticsearch
import requests
from google.cloud.storage import Client

@app.route('/')
def index():
    return {'hello': 'world'}
```
#### requirements.txt
```
boto3
connexion
elasticsearch
elasticsearch-dsl
google-cloud-storage
requests
```
#### .chalice/config.json
```
{
  "version": "2.0",
  "app_name": "timings",
  "stages": {
    "dev": {
      "api_gateway_stage": "api",
      "lambda_memory_size": 128
    }
  }
}
```
Each test is preceded by `chalice deploy` and waiting for a minute.
### Test: burst coldstart without `compileall`
#### Typical result
```

Aren't you already relaying on a black box native implementation of Kerberos on Windows? Is there really anything wrong with using platform APIs?

The thing is that many servers don't support plain Kerberos. They require negotiate which can resolve to either Kerberos or NTLM. Supporting negotiate and ntlm here will make this work transparently, and I think also allows for Windows SSO even for NTLM if you use SSPI to implement this (Which should already happen for Kerberos if you use SSPI).

I think requests_ntlm is pure Python and as such can't support Windows SSO.

This would be nice to have! However, Requests doesn't support multiple auth handlers, so it can be a problem if you want to use one auth method for the proxy, and another for the actual request. This is already an issue with HttpNtlmAuth from requests-ntlm, though I don't know if the problem's tracked anywhere.

HttpNtlmAuth also handles NTLM authentication for either a proxy or a website, based on what response header it sees first, which makes it a bit of an oddball.

For reference in case somebody is stuck at my situation - what I've ended up doing is this:
```python
    partial_rows_data = table.read_rows(
            start_key=start_key,
            end_key=end_key,
            limit=limit,
            filter_=filter_,
            end_inclusive = end_inclusive
    )
    for row in partial_rows_data._generator.read_rows():
        yield ( row.row_key, row )
```

`partial_rows_data._generator` is a `YieldRowsData` so besides the access to the 'private' attribute, it's pretty much the same as `yield_rows()`.

> SAM will never change the resource naming structure

I also found out, that the logical ID of a stage is the concatenation `logicalIdOfRestApi+stageName+"Stage"`.
However **since today I experience, that the naming structure has changed** to `logicalIdOfRestApi+"Stage"`.

This is why the following works for me. My Api has the logical name `Api` and I use DependsOn with `ApiStage`:
```yaml
...
Parameters:
  ApiKeyId:
    Description: "The ID of an existing ApiKey"
    Type: String
  ApiName:
    Description: "The Name of the Api"
    Type: String
  StageName:
    Description: "The stage to be deployed to"
    Type: String
    Default: production

Resources:
  FooFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
...

  Api:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      Name: !Ref ApiName
      StageName: !Ref StageName
      DefinitionBody:
        swagger: "2.0"
        schemes:
        - "https"
        paths:
          '/scaled':
            get:
              responses: {}
              x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
                uri: !Sub arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${FooFunction.Arn}/invocations
                passthroughBehavior: "when_no_match"
                httpMethod: "POST"
                type: aws_proxy
              security:
                - api_key: []
        securityDefinitions:
          api_key:
            type: "apiKey"
            name: "x-api-key"
            in: "header"
        x-amazon-apigateway-api-key-source : "HEADER"

  ServiceUsagePlan:
    Type: "AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlan"
    DependsOn: ApiStage
    Properties:
      ApiStages:
        - ApiId:
            Ref: Api
          Stage: !Ref StageName
  ServiceUsagePlanKey:
    Type: "AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlanKey"
    DependsOn: ServiceUsagePlan
    Properties :
      KeyId: !Ref ApiKeyId
      KeyType: API_KEY
      UsagePlanId:
        Ref: ServiceUsagePlan
...
```

```yaml
StreamName: !Select [2, !Split ["/", !GetAtt Table.StreamArn]]

@OriHoch This is not about dumping to S3 but loading a resource from a private S3. Eg: my datapackage,json might look like this
```
{
  name: my-datapackage
  resources: [
    name: my-resource
    path: data/data.csv
    schema: {...}
  ]
}
```
And that dp.json and resource are stored in my private bucket:
* https://amazonaws.com/my.private.bucket/my-datapackage/datapackage.json
* https://amazonaws.com/my.private.bucket/my-datapackage/data/data.csv

Now if I want to save `data.csv` locally I would  normally  run:

```
my-pipeline:
  - 
    run: load_resource
    parameters: 
      url: s3-signed-url-for(https://amazonaws.com/my.private.bucket/my-datapackage/datapackage.json)
  - run: stream_remote_resources
  - run: dump.to_path  
```

But this will fail as when dpp will try to load https://amazonaws.com/my.private.bucket/my-datapackage/data/data.csv it will get `403 forbidden`

This PR will allow me to add custom `load_resource` where I can use `process_datapackage` method to generate pre-signed url for resources first and after continue regular process, That will use that URLs to load resources (avoiding `403 forbidden`)

Under the hood in SoS, we essentially only communicate between tasks and the "master" process the pass/fail status determined by shell return code. That is the only think kept. We leave status per job written to a hashed file and the `sos status` command is used to retrieve it, and even make some summary plots (feature of SoS Notebook)! This design is adopted because it is more general to where a job is run: you can imaging it is easy when all are run on the same computer and all these info get exchanged in memory. But we also run distributed jobs to multiple nodes on cluster so in-memory communication is more difficult.

Pushing forward on the thinking in which we do `globus transfer share *`.

In terms of modeling it as a separate command, I think that all of the endpoint manipulation commands that are duplicated (like deletion) just need a little bit of special-casing to print a message directing users to do the "right thing" next time.
Example:
```shell
$ globus transfer endpoint delete --endpoint-id abc123
warning:
  abc123 was a shared endpoint.
  We recommend deleting shared endpoints with
    globus transfer share delete --share-id abc123

Successfully deleted Endpoint abc123 (MyFancyShareDisplayName)
```

So warn, but still proceed with the operation.

Related question: should `globus transfer share *` commands use `--endpoint-id` or a new param like `--share-id`?
I think it should be `--share-id` to reinforce the notion that a Share is a first-class entity.
Docs can state that `--share-id` is an `--endpoint-id` under the hood.

Some of my internal bits and pieces for handling endpoints (like my fancy [create/update args decorator](https://github.com/globus/globus-cli/blob/master/globus_cli/services/transfer/endpoint/helpers.py)) are decently portable with minimal effort to this model, which I like.

> Same signal.

I do not agree with you. At least for the DFT, these are not equal. You can interpret the signals as
- `x2` is `x1` with doubled sampling rate ( e.g. 5Hz vs 10Hz) OR
- with both sampling rates being equal (5Hz) you observed `x2` for 2 seconds and `x1` for 1 second, and the signals are different

However, if you do normalization with `n`, you can at least compare the 0th coefficient, which is the sum of the signal values, being a somehow trivial feature (and possibly duplicated by *tsfresh*).

```python
np.abs(np.fft.rfft(x1) / 5)
# Out: array([ 0.5       ,  0.2126627 ,  0.13143278])
np.abs(np.abs(np.fft.rfft(x2) / 10))
# Out: array([ 0.5       ,  0.17978155,  0.09451676,  0.06867044,  0.05841457,  0.05555556])
```
but sadly, using `norm="ortho"` (which is 1/sqrt(n)), there is no exploitable information

```python
np.abs(np.fft.rfft(x1, norm="ortho"))
# Out: array([ 1.11803399,  0.47552826,  0.29389263])
np.abs(np.fft.rfft(x2, norm="ortho"))
# Out: array([ 1.58113883,  0.56851919,  0.29888823,  0.21715501,  0.18472308,
#         0.17568209])
```
at least, normalization is valuable if you compare time series with the same length:

```python
y1 = np.linspace(0,1,5)

y2 = np.linspace(0,2,5)

np.fft.rfft(y1, norm="ortho")
# Out: array([ 1.11803399+0.j        , -0.27950850+0.38471044j,
#        -0.27950850+0.09081782j])
np.fft.rfft(y2, norm="ortho")
# Out: array([ 2.23606798+0.j        , -0.55901699+0.76942088j,
#        -0.55901699+0.18163563j])
```

Yes, agree! It should be well-discussed and planned before we start.

We can plan for archival nodes first. Some primary difference between `VM` and `ShardVM` may be:
1. Different data structure between block and collation
	1. Different `configure_header` functions
	2. Some methods look reusable, we need to decide which of them need to rewrite.
		* For example:`block.parent_hash` and `collation.parent_collation_hash` are really alike but also meaningful. (I think it’s for distinguishing with `period_start_prevhash`). 
	3. Shard chain uses main chain’s **Blockchain opcodes**- `blockhash, coinbase, timestamp, block_number, difficulty, and gas_limit`
		* I noted in #247 TODOs: Decouple `VMState.block_header` the `VMState` properties (`blockhash, coinbase, timestamp, block_number, difficulty, and gas_limit)`, because shard chains use `period_start_prevhash` block properties of main chain in contracts, but `VMState.state_db()` should use `VMState.collation_header's state_root`.
		* It can be modified in `VM` to keep the codes consistent but will be unnecessary for main chain.
2. No PoW for shard
	1. No `uncle`  and `nephew` rewards
	2. No `compute_difficulty`, only simple score increment. (`collation_number` ==collation score)


Also, I think we can benefit from drawing the whole JSON-RPC transaction sequence diagram between shard client and main chain. It could help us to figure out when/where should we call for the data from main chain.

@jasongrout, new pull request.  I misread the docs too.  ```ssl_verify``` can now take an arbitrary value.

@kalefranz  I am not sure if this is the right solution, but the following patch fixes this issue for me:
```patch
@@ -103,8 +102,10 @@ class Context(Configuration):
     # remote connection details
     ssl_verify = PrimitiveParameter(True, element_type=string_types + (bool,),
                                     validation=ssl_verify_validation)
-    client_ssl_cert = PrimitiveParameter('', aliases=('client_cert',))
-    client_ssl_cert_key = PrimitiveParameter('', aliases=('client_cert_key',))
+    client_ssl_cert = PrimitiveParameter(None, aliases=('client_cert',),
+                                         element_type=string_types + (NoneType,))
+    client_ssl_cert_key = PrimitiveParameter(None, aliases=('client_cert_key',),
+                                             element_type=string_types + (NoneType,))

> Based on the way conda asks for the information, A-Cloud has to iterate over 5272 packages, and 100,000 files in those 5000 packages to gather the requested information. (for every anaconda install command - even if the request is fulfilled by repo.continuum.io

What? How is this the case? All it is doing is asking for `repodata.json.bz2`, once per channel. Why is Anaconda Cloud iterating over all of these packages when this one file is being requested?

For repo.continuum.io in particular, this is a _static file_. There's no way it is iterating over all of the packages in defaults to get this information.

I could definitely see `conda` doing a better job of relying on its internal cache. As far as I can tell, `conda` always asks for a new copy of each channel's `repodata.json.bz2` file with every conda command unless `--use-cache` is specified. We could do a much smaller query of the modification time for the `repodata` file and compare it to the modification time of the local cached copy.

The available installation mechanisms seem to carry some undesirable characteristics for the build server scenario. Here are characteristics that are important to us:

Here's where all this came from, when I was first putting together a tutorial that details how to load balance VMs - how we add and remove from LB now - https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/linux/tutorial-load-balancer#add-and-remove-vms

I'm totally glossing over the fact that you already know/assume myNic**2** is attached to myVM**2**. Otherwise I need to query the VM to find out which NIC is connected, and then pass that NIC name or ID.

Yes, you can have VMs with multiple NICs if the CLI does this under the hood for you. And yes, NICs can be long standing resources whereas the VM resource itself could be torn down and a new one stood up :) But, for the quick-ish start scenario where folks just want two or three VMS behind a load balancer so they can test how their app responds, can make the case they'd only have one NIC per VM.

So I'm cool with not adding to bloat of `az vm create`, but at least the CLI doing the lookup of NIC to VM for those load balancer operations would be sweet. That way I have a few commands to build the LB, use `az vm create` to prop my vnet, subnet, and NSG, and then just attach my _VM_ to the LB without worrying about what NIC was created or having to create all those networking resources first and _then_ attach the NIC to the VM.

Is that a good middle ground? `az network lb backend-pool add-vm` or `az network lb backend-pool remove-vm` where the CLI looks up the NIC mapping based on the VM provided?

Commands in Azure 2.0 are run using `az`.  Commands in Xplat are run using `azure`.  The parameters you listed:

```
/usr/local/bin/azure vm create
--resource-group
--name
--location
--vm-size
--nic-names
--storage-account-name
--storage-account-container-name
--admin-username
--admin-password
--image-urn
--plan-name
--plan-publisher
--plan-product
--os-type
```

These are parameters used in Xplat, not Azure CLI 2.0.  You have installed it correctly, but the thing you are comparing it to isn't the older version of Azure CLI 2.0, but rather Xplat CLI.

Yup, you're right that if you don't explicitly request a Python version, it "defaults" to the currently-running Python to create the virtualenv (since that's the Python virtualenv runs as).

Our recommendation for how to handle this is to specifically request a Python version using `language_version`. Here's an example from one of our `.pre-commit-config.yaml` files:

```yaml
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    sha: v0.7.1
    hooks:
    -   id: trailing-whitespace
        exclude: ^assets/vendor/
        language_version: python3.6
    -   id: end-of-file-fixer
        exclude: ^assets/vendor/
        language_version: python3.6
    -   id: autopep8-wrapper
        language_version: python3.6
    -   id: check-json
        files: \.(jshintrc|json)$
        language_version: python3.6
    -   id: check-yaml
        language_version: python3.6
```

In general, I think our recommendation is to specify a `language_version` on basically every hook. Otherwise you can get confusing behavior when the hooks were first run with a different Python version. (Some hooks, like `check-yaml`, don't really matter -- any version of Python should be able to run the hook with the same results -- but the ones that e.g. parse the AST matter a lot.)

I know copying and pasting the language_version after every hook isn't great. This is one of the problems with the current config format, but unfortunately we can't really fix it in a backwards-compatible way (since the top-level is a list). Eventually we're going to take the breaking change in the config format and will have a way to specify a project-wide default for language versions.

This is how i've worked around it for the time being:
```
touch hosts/production/secret-vars.yml
echo "#prod vars" >> hosts/production/secret-vars.yml
git add hosts/production/secret-vars.yml
git commit -m "add production secret vars"

echo 'hosts/production/secret-vars.yml filter=git-crypt diff=git-crypt' >> .gitattributes
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "add production secret vars"

git push
```

@danielrobbins as you could probably have guessed I have downloaded files which take more than 5 minutes in the past, I'm well aware it's over http.

The insinuation that I didn't know that together with your comments on #2249 are not necessary and more to the point, not an effective way of arguing for a change in aiohttp.

## My take on this

### Docs

The use of `async with async_timeout.timeout(10):` in the client example at the top of the aiohttp docs is unnecessary and misleading - it indicates you need to use the timeout context which you don't.

Since some other libraries don't have a default timeout we should be very explicit about the fact that aiohttp has a timeout and how to override it.

Not strictly docs, but: the timeout exception is currently very unhelpful, just:

```
...
  File "/home/samuel/code/mithra/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/helpers.py", line 661, in __exit__
    raise asyncio.TimeoutError from None
concurrent.futures._base.TimeoutError
```

This timeout should have a message saying "request read reached it's timeout of 300s and was cancelled, use read_timeout to override this" or something.

### The default timeout

While of course http requests come in all shapes and sizes and aren't elegantly grouped into different types, you could roughly categorize http requests into two sorts: 
  1. pseudo instant (to a human) website requests and also webhooks type requests and most restful api requests. These requests generally send <1MB and receive less than <10MB. Many systems (eg. Heroku) limit requests like this to 30seconds.
  2. "Data downloads" where an end user might expect a progress bar. These often consist of binrary data, can be of arbitrary size and should have no timeout, a very large timeout or custom timeout logic set by the application developer.

With this thinking, the use of 300s timeout is an odd choice. For me a 30s default timeout would make more sense, this would also mean unexpected failures happened more quickly and would therefore be more likely to be found quickly.

### asyncio task exceptions confuse people

My suspicion is that developers new to asyncio are often confused by the way that exceptions inside tasks aren't raised or shown anywhere - they're "hidden" and only raised when `task.result()` is called. This still get's me occasionally: "what's happened to that task? Oh, now I've got the result and an error which occurred an hour ago has now blown up".

While this is a python feature and not specific to aiohttp, perhaps we could better guard against this confusion using warnings or logging on client requests?

👍 extensions be enable-able by default.

+0 to YAML. I love writing YAML (you'd better, if you use conda), and see it as the best editor UI for a JSON-based model... like the "edit metadata" dialog in the notebook would be more usable if it spoke (collapsible, linted, schema'd) YAML. But I do like the portability/inspectability of JSON on disk, despite its diffability handicap. Given that pyzmq, one of the few c upstreams, _still_ creates problems occasionally, i am not clamoring to add it as a hard dependency... even though it's in `conda`!

As [mentioned](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/2894#issuecomment-333834180) over on #2894, another hat in the ring would be an `entry_point`-based way to put things into the config. For a "pure" javascript nbextension, this python-based approach might not be as useful... but packaging `extension.yaml` and `index.js` is no problem on conda (assuming a recipe someplace) and presumably you can have a data-files-only wheel (assuming a setup.py)? That doesn't seem like much maintainer overhead to let users use the tools they trust for their compute tasks :) `npm` still scares me.

Anyhow, however the baseline config gets on disk, I am very 👍 on there being a user-serviceable,  recoverable, discoverable, beautiful way to manipulate the state of the javascript application for both notebook and lab.

**user-serviceable and recoverable**

I love the CLI, but think something powerful and visual like atom or `configurator` seems really nice. 

At it's simplest: Run configurator, whether it's in the notebook, (or next to the notebook), and you configure the state of the javascript application, reload the page, and you're good. Better, keep the versions around so you can go back to them in case of fail (so don't make the configurator an nbextension, i guess?).

**discoverable**

The package managers don't make this particularly easy (i.e. can't search for `entry_points` or [namespaces](https://packaging.python.org/guides/packaging-namespace-packages/), magic names are weak, etc.), so the "big box of extensions" packages like `contrib` or other/future ones (e.g. genomics, CAD) are valuable for their curation purposes, as if they leverage common licensing, documentation, code review, testing, etc. they are a maintenance win vs many small repos, even if they don't specifically share a common focus.

For the case of, "we'll ship this, but you might not want it", it seems like shipping the assets, but with an in-env baseline would let you have both.
```yaml
load_nbextensions:
    thing-everyone-wants: TRUE
    other-thing-everyone-wants: y
    thing-some-people-want: no
    thing-b: off
```
> The above is a playful illustration of how YAML's "human-editability" can get a little [out of hand](http://yaml.org/type/bool.html). 

Anyhow, back to packaging `contrib`: One could probably apply automation akin to `lerna` to the build of these with wheel/conda, and get a metapackage and _n_ separately-versioned packages, all specified by the version of the "contrib" metapackage... but if the dependencies of all of them are reasonable, that might be a bunch of work for no real gain... unless its for piecemeal upstreaming into a domain-specific pack, as suggested above. Specifically on things that are possible with nbconvert, you can quickly rack up some expensive ones! 

**beautiful**

Perhaps, looking at the metadata discussion above, the right on-disk metadata and documentation format is... the notebook! I think if an extension packed a well-named notebook, with some metadata (visually edited and previewed by the configurator), we'd be able leverage all of the existing machinery and let people make some really nice stuff.

So in `extension.ipynb` (or whatever), use some (visually-defined) tags, `jupyter-extension:name`, `jupyter-extension:screenshots`, etc. Parse the metadata and serve them up as some sandboxed HTML.

Your code design might look a bit like that of [Kayak](https://github.com/HIPS/Kayak), which was a predecessor to autograd in the HIPS group. In particular, it might be helpful to glance at the [MatElemMult](https://github.com/HIPS/Kayak/blob/b8d0eca9eb4cd17e965251dcc5ed97933e49f7d8/kayak/matrix_ops.py#L113-L156) class (since there's no `MatElemDiv` class afaict). I don't think anyone is actively maintaining Kayak but I thought it couldn't hurt as inspiration.

That sounds right to me about JITing (Julia with a [DualNumber type](https://github.com/mattjj/autodiff/blob/master/forward_mode.jl) would do that right now). But even a big constant-factor speedup from compilation probably can't make up for the algorithmic disadvantage of forward mode (for the use cases I'm thinking of).

I think the main autograd overhead is exactly what you say: building and then traversing the computation graph, which is kind of like running an interpreter within Python's VM. Other people have brought up wanting to apply some kind of existing Python JIT tech to autograd (e.g. #47) but really we'd have to first compile autograd's representation to one of these languages (e.g. Python or numba). (Actually, since control flow might change the computation graph from execution to execution, even that would only apply to computation graphs that don't change from evaluation to evaluation, though that's a nice special case to handle.)

The others might be able to give you better refs but I get the impression that the gold standard is [Barak Pearlmutter's work](http://www.bcl.hamilton.ie/~barak/publications.html), like maybe [this brief intro](http://arxiv.org/pdf/1404.7456v1.pdf). I don't know of benchmarks, but the gap between forward-mode and reverse-mode can be made arbitrarily large just by scaling the problem dimensions, so any constant-factor implementation differences (like benchmarks would test) probably wouldn't be very enlightening.

  - `rpc_get_all_names()`
    - change pagination from 100 names to 50, or 25, or 10 (needs evaluation; see #701)
    - 100-name page size takes ~500 milliseconds to process
  - `rpc_getinfo()`, `rpc_ping()`, `success_response()`
    - Cache bitcoind block height and consensus hash to avoid DB read (#702)
    - can keep coherent via `index_blockchain()` thread
  - `rpc_get_name_blockchain_record()`
    - Returns name history as well, so O(n) in number of name transactions
    - Add complementary RPC method that does NOT return history (#716)
      - affects `GET /v1/users`
      - affects `POST /v1/names`
      - affects `GET /v1/names/{:name}/zonefile`
      - probably more
  - `rpc_get_name_at()`, `rpc_get_nameops_affected_at()`
    - Very slow in `master`, since it replays history (O(n))
    - Much faster in `feature/clean-chainstate`, since it leverages new chainstate DB in virtualchain’s `develop` branch (O(1))

> What do you (@jvivian) and @arkal define "a pipeline" to be?

My operating definition is that a pipeline = a workflow = a stitched together DAG of jobs. Given that definition, I don't think of STAR or BWA or etc as jobs because they're composed of multiple different jobs (e.g., download_sample, download_indices, align_reads, etc).

We have the same definition.  STAR and BWA aren't part of a pipeline until someone does the wiring.  They can be defined as stand alone functions with inputs that are pipeline-independent, with a return statement for the output of STAR that is also pipeline generic. 

> Do you plan to have the aligners in aligners.py implement a common interface? 

I was striving for arguments to the job functions that are pipeline independent. Generally, I've been making the arguments be FileStoreIDs of the inputs and nothing else.  No vague / enigmatic "config" or "inputs" objects/dicts.   E.G.

```
def index_bam(job, bam_id):
    """
    Runs samtools index to create (.bai) files

    :param JobFunctionWrappingJob job: passed automatically by Toil
    :param str bam_id: FileStoreID of the bam file
    :return: BAM index FileStoreID
    :rtype: str
    """
    work_dir = job.fileStore.getLocalTempDir()
    job.fileStore.readGlobalFile(bam_id, os.path.join(work_dir, 'sample.bam'))
    # Call: index the bam
    parameters = ['index', '/data/sample.bam']
    docker_call(work_dir=work_dir, parameters=parameters,
                tool='quay.io/ucsc_cgl/samtools:0.1.19--dd5ac549b95eb3e5d166a5e310417ef13651994e')
    return job.fileStore.writeGlobalFile(os.path.join(work_dir, 'sample.bam.bai'))
```

```python
@pytest.yield_fixture
def event_loop():
    """Create an instance of the default event loop for each test case."""
    policy = asyncio.get_event_loop_policy()
    res = policy.new_event_loop()
    asyncio.set_event_loop(res)
    res._close = res.close
    res.close = lambda: None

    yield res

    res._close()
```

```python
@total_ordering
class CustomEvent(dict):
    def __lt__(self, other):
        return self['date'] < other['date']

class CustomEventWrapper(object):
    def __init__(self, event_data):
        self.event_data = event_data
    def __call__(self, date):
        return CustomEvent({'title': self.event_data.get('title', None), 'date': date})


class SkippingRRule(rrule):
    def __init__(self, wrapper, *args, exc=[], **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.wrapper = wrapper
        self.exc = exc
    def __iter__(self):
        self._my_iter = enumerate(super().__iter__())
        return self
    def __next__(self):
        i, value = next(self._my_iter)
        while i in self.exc:
            i, value = next(self._my_iter)
        return self.wrapper(value)


wrapper1 = CustomEventWrapper({'title': 'event1'})
wrapper2 = CustomEventWrapper({'title': 'event2'})
s = rruleset()
s.rrule(SkippingRRule(wrapper1, DAILY, count=10, dtstart=parse("19970902T090000"), exc=[3]))
s.rrule(SkippingRRule(wrapper2, DAILY, count=10, dtstart=parse("19970903T090000")))
list(s)
```

> Unless specifying force_load or force_dump implies that any unlisted fields shouldn't be loaded or dumped.

No, those options wouldn't solve the issue of whitelisting deserialized fields.

The option currently used to whitelist a set of fields on a schema is `fields`, but [it only applies to serialization](https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html#marshmallow.Schema.Meta), not deserialization, which seems a bit asymmetrical considering there is nothing about the name "fields" that implies serialization exclusively.

> a user of marshmallow had a large schema class, with many fields, but only wanted 1 of those fields to be deserializable

It looks like [partial loading](https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html#partial-loading) ([the `partial` option for `Schema.load()`](https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference.html#marshmallow.Schema.load)) might be an existing solution. The "partial" white list has to be supplied on each `load()`, which doesn't address the concern of "forgetting to put [it] in", but the set of fields could be assigned to a variable for reuse.

```py
deserializable_fields = ('fieldname',)
...
data, errors = schema.load(partial=deserializable_fields)
```

If the white list really needs to be declared on a schema class or instance, maybe a separate schema class should be declared.

The positional args on `Decimal.__init__` are: `places`, `rounding`, `default`, `as_string`. However, on `Number.__init__` (and for `Integer` and `Float`), the args are: `default`, `attribute`, `as_string`, `error`.

It's a pretty minor mismatch - the only issue is that `as_string` is re-ordered relative to `attribute`. On the other hand, I can't imagine why any sane person would pass `as_string` in as a positional arg, so this is a non-issue.

It only matters in that maybe you'd want to drop `as_string` as an explicit arg on `Decimal.__init__` and just have it handled via `args` or `kwargs`, but this has a slight chance of changing the existing behavior, if anybody is doing the silly thing of passing in `as_string` as a positional arg here.

Thanks, I get it now. In webargs it expects to assemble the data (which might be from the query string) into a dict that can then be loaded into a Schema. But marshmallow-jsonapi expects the data to be in a very specific arrangement.
To work around this I did something like this:
```python
import marshmallow as ma
from marshmallow import fields as ma_fields
import marshmallow_jsonapi as majapi
import webargs.core
import webargs.flaskparser


class JsonApiNested(ma.Schema):
    """ Placeholder wrapper for webargs to marshmallow-jsonapi handling. """
    # webargs.flaskparser.FlaskParser specifically returns missing if there is no json
    # at all instead of returning the field default
    data = ma_fields.Dict(required=True)  #, missing={}, default={})
    class Meta:  # pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods
        """ Sets strict so an error will be raised when invalid data is passed in. """
        strict = True


class JsonApiParser(webargs.flaskparser.FlaskParser):
    """ Special case handling for marshmallow-jsonapi.Schema's """
    def _parse_request(self, schema, req, locations):
        if isinstance(schema, majapi.Schema):
            jsonapi = JsonApiNested()
            data = super()._parse_request(jsonapi, req, locations)
            if data['data'] == webargs.core.missing:
                data = {}
        else:
            data = super()._parse_request(schema, req, locations)

        return data or {}

```

Serial instances can not be pickled and they can not be shared across processes as the underlying OS resources are not that easily transferable (may depend on OS and process invocation method, though noting is explicitly supported by pySerial).

You can retrieve and apply the settings of an instance using [get_settings](https://pyserial.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyserial_api.html#serial.Serial.get_settings) and [apply_settings](https://pyserial.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyserial_api.html#serial.Serial.apply_settings). But this does not open a port on itself.

Opening the same port with multiple processes at the same time is not supported across all OS. For a portable solution, use one process servicing the port and inter process communication to talk with the others.

> Frankly, I fail to see how the change permission for read actions increases security as at the same time this permission also allows PUT. Mixing permissions for different actions doesn't seem a good choice.

We know that if a user can change something, then they can read it, that's how the Django admin works.

This version is more secure, because it forces the developer to think about what they're doing. If instead of inventing their own "read" permission, the developer chooses to deliberately give everyone "change" permissions when they should only have "read" permissions, that's their problem; I can't stop other developers from deliberately doing stupid things, I'm here to stop Tastypie from doing stupid things. The point of this change was to prevent global read permissions on any resource that used DjangoAuthorization, which developers might not expect; the new behavior is in line with what developers experience in the Django admin.

If you want the old behavior:
1. Don't upgrade to 0.13.2.
2. Or override the `read_list and `read_detail` methods.

If you think the documentation could be improved, feel free to submit a PR.

The error you report indicates that "TRACE" is defined from "trace.h", but trace.cpp isn't compiled with the same flag.

Great, thanks! To get others up to speed: The main goal would be to have a working Hessian-free implementation done completely in Theano. This requires a Conjugate Gradient implementation with backtracking. What Carlos implemented in that gist is something following the interface of `lasagne.updates.*`, with a little twist: The update dictionary doesn't just do a single step, but uses `theano.scan` to run Conjugate Gradient until convergence and return the end result.

While discussing the implementation with Carlos, I wondered if this should split up a bit further. I was thinking of something like:
* `lasagne.updates.cg_step`: Return an update dictionary for a single CG step
* `lasagne.updates.until_convergence`: Use `theano.scan` to apply some update repeatedly until either a given stopping criterion or a maximum number of iterations is reached, optionally with back-tracking with a second loss
* `lasagne.updates.cg`: Convenience function to apply `cg_step` until convergence with a suitable stopping criterion, optionally with back-tracking
* `lasagne.updates.hf`: Implementation of Hessian-free that uses `cg_step` `until_convergence` by default, but could use something else

Separating out `cg_step` and `until_convergence` would allow to track progress of CG while it's running, and allow `until_convergence` to be used with other optimizers and stopping criteria. In theory, you could implement mini-batched SGD in a single scan (but probably that won't buy you anything).

What we need to think through and decide:

1. Do we want this, and is this a good separation? (I think yes and yes.)
2. What exactly would the interface of the different functions look like? (`until_convergence` would probably be the most complex, as it may need some callback for defining the stopping criterion expression. `hf` would also be complex, as it usually uses separate streams of mini-batches for the outer loop and the inner (cg) loop.)

Let's quickly check whether this is really what we want. There are several options:
1. Ensuring the correct broadcast pattern in the `InputLayer` (as I commented above, this is not a good solution)
2. Ensuring the correct broadcast pattern in `lasagne.layers.get_output` when collecting the inputs (it further complicates the `get_output` function, but what can you do...)
3. Ensuring the correct broadcast pattern in `MergeLayer`

If we go for solution 2, do we still need solution 3 or can we rely on the MergeLayer getting correctly broadcastable tensors?

If we go for solution 3, we don't need a `self.broadcastable` attribute. `get_output_for()` can access `self.input_shapes` to compute the broadcast patterns on the fly. I think this is cleaner.

No matter whether we choose solution 2 or 3, I'd suggest a `lasagne.utils.ensure_broadcasts(var, shape)` function encapsulating the steps: (1) infer the broadcast pattern from the shape, (2) compare it to the broadcast pattern of the variable, (3) add a T.patternbroadcast if the patterns do not match.

Eventually I got some time for this thread!

I'm planning to make the following improvements to our CFG recovery.

Performance:
- It will try to resolve indirect-jumps less often. Generally it helps a lot in some sexy architectures like MIPS, where most functions calls are compiled down to indirect jumps.
- Create a `CFGFast` analysis routine, which does some preliminary CFG recovery tasks with the help of function prolog/epilog identification, jump table recovery, etc. It does not rely on `SimState`s, which is why it's fairly fast (and is usually comparable to IDA's CFG). It's less accurate, of course, but it should be enough for a preliminary understanding. The current `CFG` will become `CFGAccurate`.
- I'll see if it's possible to make CFG recovery multi-threaded. Using only one core on a 40-core machine is a crime.

Code:
- I'm refactoring CFG's code. Personally I'm not a code neat freak (unlike someone else in the team), and over time, the `CFG` class has bit-rotten quite a little. And I have to admit that some of my old C-style code is really difficult to understand (even for myself!) My goal is to make CFG code more modular and easy to read. It's difficult, but I'll try my best.
- Logging. It's a big issue right now for users that CFG spits quite some error logs, most of which are false alarms. For those true alarms, very few people really understands how to deal with it. "No bytes in memory" is definitely not helpful to understand what's going on. I'll make those logging more useful to end users with more descriptions and suggestions of solutions.

Visualization:
- Right now, debugging CFG is a pain, especially for big binaries. I spend a lot of time typing `cfg_node.successors[0].successors[0]....predecessors[1]` just to understand what the graph looks like. I'm planning to develop `angr-management`, which will eventually give users a friendly interface to browse and dig into a CFG with a certain level of context-sensitivity.

Hi, thanks for the comment.

The reason why you're getting different results between the .query and .querymany functions for the same input list is because some of your inputs aren't listed as "symbol" fields in our database, but rather "alias" or "name" fields.  

When you do the individual .query calls for each input (note that "scopes" is not an accepted parameter for .query - it ends up being ignored at the server), the query string (e.g. "DDX26B") is searched against the Elasticsearch "\_all\_" field (which includes "alias" and other fields), matching the documents you've shown above (ENSG00000225235 and ENSG00000165359).

However, when you do the .querymany, you're searching _only_ against the "symbol" field (which doesn't have a match for the query string "DDX26B" (as this is an "alias").  The shortest answer is to add "alias" and "name" to your scopes, then you'll get the results you expect, as in the following:

```python

In [1]: from mygene import MyGeneInfo

In [2]: mg = MyGeneInfo()

In [3]: genes = ['DDX26B',
   ...: 'CCDC83',
   ...: 'MAST3',
   ...: 'RPL11',
   ...: 'ZDHHC20',
   ...: 'LUC7L3',
   ...: 'SNORD49A',
   ...: 'CTSH',
   ...: 'ACOT8']

In [4]: for gene in genes:
   ...:     result = mg.query(gene, fields=["ensembl"], species="human", verbose=False)
   ...:     hgnc_name = gene
   ...:     for hit in result['hits']:
   ...:         if "ensembl" in hit and "gene" in hit["ensembl"]:
   ...:             print("{}\t{}".format(hgnc_name, hit["ensembl"]["gene"]))
   ...:             
DDX26B	ENSG00000225235
DDX26B	ENSG00000165359
CCDC83	ENSG00000150676
MAST3	ENSG00000099308
RPL11	ENSG00000142676
ZDHHC20	ENSG00000180776
ZDHHC20	ENSG00000236953
LUC7L3	ENSG00000108848
SNORD49A	ENSG00000277370
CTSH	ENSG00000103811
ACOT8	ENSG00000101473

In [5]: results = mg.querymany(genes, scopes=["symbol","alias","name"], fields=["ensembl"], species="human", verbose=False)

In [6]: for res in results:
   ...:     hgnc_name = res['query']
   ...:     if "ensembl" in res and "gene" in res["ensembl"]:
   ...:         print("{}\t{}".format(hgnc_name, res["ensembl"]["gene"]))
   ...:         
DDX26B	ENSG00000165359
DDX26B	ENSG00000225235
CCDC83	ENSG00000150676
MAST3	ENSG00000099308
RPL11	ENSG00000142676
ZDHHC20	ENSG00000180776
ZDHHC20	ENSG00000236953
LUC7L3	ENSG00000108848
SNORD49A	ENSG00000277370
CTSH	ENSG00000103811
ACOT8	ENSG00000101473
```

Hope that makes sense, let us know if you have any other questions.

The PR #401 implements a method `vehicle.wait_ready(attr1, attr2, ..., timeout=30, raise_exception=True)`

From the initialization of the vehicle, we keep an ongoing list of what attributes have been emitted via the notification listener. wait_ready sees if those have been triggered, and if not, waits for them (up to a timeout, which can be None). For example:

1. `await_param` behavior is now expressed in terms of `wait_ready('gps_0', 'parameters')`.
2. If I have downloaded all parameters already, `wait_ready('parameters')` would return instantly.

etc. Note that certain attributes can become un-ready, or expire, such as "commands" and e.g. "link_alive" (if it were implemented). These are removed for the attribute record and we wait for the notification to be sent again in the loop. This seemed like the most intuitive behavior for invalidation.

We can also expose it as a parameter in connect(), if we want. I think the duplication is worth it. 

Idiomatically, we should also define what `connect(wait_ready=True)` does in terms of "default" values. My reasoning: we can standardize on `.commands.download(wait_ready=True)` to reduce the number of duplicate calls to download + wait_ready on the commands object. The semantics for the keyword argument would then be the same across objects and the whole codebase.

Speaking personally now, I feel that the 2nd option ("best effort" `Total-Records`) is the worst of both worlds. We pay the performance penalty, but we don't get an accurate result.

Thinking about this more, `Total-Records` is essentially always exposed to race conditions, because even if it's accurate now, there's no guarantee that it will be accurate when the client receives it, parses it, or sends another request. In other words, relying on `Total-Records` to do correct pagination is essentially always wrong; any client that relies on the first request's `Total-Records` is doomed, and even relying on the most recent request's `Total-Records` is wrong because new records could have been inserted before or after the current page. So my feeling is that we should scrap the options in category 1.

I think my current preference is something like category 3, but with a caveat that if a user does a HEAD request, we calculate the `Total-Records` correctly (this is what kinto-http.js's `getTotalRecords` function does). There are use cases where category 2 is useful, but I think such cases are better solved by forcing clients to request total records explicitly, and I think category 3 is the closest we can come without actually breaking the API.

There are two potential sources of difference, both of which are subtle implementation details.  The first is that `periodization` pads odd-length inputs up to the nearest even-length signal by replicating the last value.  For example, the following to transforms give the same coefficients:
```Python
import pywt
pywt.dwt([1, 2, 3], wavelet='db1', mode='periodization')
# (array([ 2.12132034,  4.24264069]), array([-0.70710678,  0.        ]))
pywt.dwt([1, 2, 3, 3], wavelet='db1', mode='periodization')
# (array([ 2.12132034,  4.24264069]), array([-0.70710678,  0.        ]))
```
So, for most odd-length signals `periodization` and `periodic` should not be expected to give the same coefficients!

In your case, your input signal is even and the difference comes from a different source.  For even signals you are seeing a difference that depends on the length of the digital filters.  Recall that the DWT is equivalent to a **downsampled** convolution.  Effectively, the C code that does the convolutions skips every second input sample.  For all modes except `periodization`, the convention in PyWavelets (and Matlab) is to convolve only the samples of the input occuring at **odd** indices [1, 3, 5, ...].  However **for periodization, the convolution has a starting index that may be either odd or even depending on the filter length**.

For periodization, the C code [determines a starting index](https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/52b1808c2353f1b78433e03e36301b63d3037749/pywt/_extensions/c/convolution.template.c#L44) for the convolution based on 1/2 the discrete filter length.  i.e.:
```Python
db5 = pywt.Wavelet('db5')
starting_index_for_convolution = db5.dec_len // 2
```
For db5 `starting_index_for_convolution` is 5 which is an odd sample so you see a result matching a subset of the coefficients from `periodic`.  For `db6`, `starting_index_for_convolution` is 6 which is even so you do not get exactly the same coefficients.  

If you circularly shift the coefficients in your example by 1 for the `db6` case you can see the match you were expecting again:
```Python
import pywt
import numpy as np
x=[1,3,5,7,6,4,5,2]

(a, d) = pywt.dwt(x,'db6','periodic')
print(a)
# [ 1.74398886  6.21015721  8.44740984  6.93296787  1.74398886  6.21015721
  8.44740984  6.93296787  1.74398886]

(a, d) = pywt.dwt(np.roll(x, 1),'db6','periodization')
print(a)
# [ 8.44740984  6.93296787  1.74398886  6.21015721]

```

The most common cause of this error is a failure to read the camera's calibration table, perhaps because the table is corrupted.  The common advice given is to perform a dynamic calibration.  This usually does not work when users try it though, as you yourself experienced.

Another alternative may be to use the dynamic calibration tool to reset your camera to factory default settings.

The calibration user guide says:

***********

While users calibrate devices with dynamic calibrator or through their own developed calibration tools based on Calibration API, there might be a need to read, write, and restore calibration data on the device. CustomRW Tool provides this ability to users. CustomRW Tool is included in the Calibration Tool package and is installed by default under C:\CalibrationToolAPI\2.5.2.0\bin on Windows and /usr/bin on Linux.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/41145062/43683533-eaae1ecc-9885-11e8-8c3f-dd9280db2864.png)

The –g option restores the calibration on device to default gold settings. Depending up on the type of calibration done on the device, the gold settings represents the original factory setting or the calibration setting from OEM Calibration if such a calibration is done on the device after factory calibration.

Note that for 90% ``nan`` errors, I think we should just have a separate utility function for that OR just put that kind of filtering in our example notebooks using standard pandas tools (rather than build it into the featurize_dataframe).

What do you not like about the solution for 2? I do agree with your argument about avoiding `n_jobs` in all of the constructors. One problem I could see with 2 is that we'd need some logic to check whether the number of threads used in `featurize_many` is read from the default value of the kwarg pass to that argument, the `n_jobs` class attribute, or the value a user passes to that function. Having three ways for the number of threads to be determined does sound problematic.

Should we just do nothing about `n_jobs`? All that we lose for not having the ability to set `n_jobs` as a class attribute is that `transform` will be serial, which does not seem like a big loss. Users are still free to featurize in parallel using `featurize_dataframe` and `featurize_many`, and the main use for `transform` is if users put the featurizer in a `Pipeline`. The main reason I'd put my featurizer in a Pipeline is to optimize the feature generation using `GridSearchCV` (having separate calls to compute features and run a model is not a big enough deal for me to make a `Pipeline`). GridSearchCV can test multiple parameterizations in parallel, so we don't necessarily need to parallel feature generation for that use case. Overall, having `transform` be serial and maintaining the ability for users to parallelize `featurize_dataframe` could keep many people happy.

For anyone encoutering the same problem, we've fixed it by:
- commenting out the `created` and `modified` fields in the `oauth_provider` app manually. 
- create the initial migration for our app that we have swapped in. 
- uncomment the `created` and `modified` fields again and create the second migration that adds the fields
- in your settings add some functionality to not set the `OAUTH2_PROVIDER_APPLICATION_MODEL` setting while migrating your app:
```python
if not os.getenv('UNSET_OAUTH2_PROVIDER_MODEL'):
    OAUTH2_PROVIDER_APPLICATION_MODEL = 'your_oauth_app.Oauth2Application'
```
- migrate both apps:
```bash
UNSET_OAUTH2_PROVIDER_MODEL=1 python manage.py migrate your_oauth_app --fake-initial
python manage.py migrate oauth2_provider
```

Hi Pascal,

I'm afraid you will have to move past the retrain.py example, because it
really is designed around a frozen image module with potentially cached
bottleneck values. Even if not cached, the way they are passed into the
Session.run() call that does the training prevents backprop through the
module.

There is no publicly available example, but I can offer some general advice:

- For the training graph, use hub.Module(..., trainable=True,
tags={"train"}) to get the graph version that operates batch norm in
training mode. (In retrain.py, this would conflict with the use of
continuous eval in the training graph.) Also, trainable=True brings in
REGULARIZATION_LOSSES from the hidden layers (if any); use those when you
see overfitting.

- Use data augmentation. For that, retrain.py is a workable starting point.

- Fine-tuning the whole network benefits from more sophisticated approaches
than plain SGD, esp. the use of momentum and learning rate decay. As a rule
of thumb, I'd recommend to start form the training regime in the
architecture's original paper (referenced from the module documentation),
with the initial learning rate cut by 10. Be aware that many uses of
RMSProp for image models set epsilon=1.0 (which is huge), and attenuates
the AdaGrad-style scaling.

- For performance, consider using a GPU, and/or multiple machines. If you
want to try in-graph replication for multiple GPUs, be sure to reuse one
Module object across the different towers, in order to share variables.
Also, for performance, consider training only the upper layers of the
module (e.g., by filtering trainable variables according to scope names).

Happy coding/training!

Arno

Yes, I think it makes sense to simply skip over TLVs when possible.

I think a good general rule of thumb for TLV processing is to "be liberal in what you accept". @jtrivedi Did you happen to fix this issue? I am stuck in a similar situation.

You can have two different experiments push data to the same plot by having both experiments use the same values for `env` and `win` as input into `vis.line`, `vis.scatter`, etc.

The behavior you describe is somewhat tricky to implement right now though, because the Python client does not maintain state, so there is no way for experiment 2 to retrieve data pushed by experiment 1 (or vice versa). The server does maintain state (for instance, state is retrieved [here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/server.py#L496)), but I don't think it is currently exposed to the user via the client. So I think you'd have to add a special handler to the server that returns state, expose this handler by adding a function in the Python client, and use explicitly alter the state in your experimental code in a way that leads to the desired behavior.

I also would like to do this!

I think it would make most sense to return a `py::array` from the function to be vectorized.

I tried this with:

```c++
py::array_t<double> return_array(double t) {
  py::array_t<double> a({2});
  a.mutable_at(0) = a.mutable_at(1) = t;
  return a;
}
```

... and:

```c++
m.def("return_array", py::vectorize(&return_array));
```

... but this created a very long compiler error, boiling down to `couldn't deduce template parameter ‘T’` and pointing to this code:

https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/2d0507db43cd5a117f7843e053b17dffca114107/include/pybind11/numpy.h#L1497-L1502

So it looks like this isn't supported (but it would be great if it would be supported!).

I tried if this works in NumPy ...

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> def myfunc(x):
...     return np.array([x, x])
... 
>>> v = np.vectorize(myfunc)
>>> v([2, 3])
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
```

... and it turns out that it doesn't!

But then I had a look at the [vectorize docs](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.vectorize.html) and found out that there is a quite new (version 1.12, released about a year ago) argument called `signature` (introduced in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/8054) that allows me to do what I want:

```pycon
>>> v = np.vectorize(myfunc, signature='()->(n)')
>>> v([2, 3])
array([[2, 2],
       [3, 3]])
```

To implement this in pybind11, it wouldn't even be necessary to add a separate argument, because the compiler would know that the return type of the function is a `py::array`.

Sadly, I don't understand enough about the internals of pybind11 to tackle an implementation myself.

It seems that this is part of a grander scheme called [Generalized Universal Functions](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/c-api.generalized-ufuncs.html).
I think it would already be great to just support `py::array`s in return values, but this could be extended to input arguments, too.

This may or may not be related to [this comment](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/issues/89#issuecomment-181908731) by @JohanMabille.

BTW, this is related to a more complicated problem I asked about on stackoverflow: https://stackoverflow.com/q/48736838/.

Two reasons: first `^` is basically the unaligned version of the default `@`, but more importantly, `^` lets the numpy-specific `g`, `F`, `D`, and `G` through.  (Without it, `g` isn't supported at all, and the capital complex ones need to be `Zf`, `Zd`).

@pconrad-fb I don’t think the real issue here is a lack of documentation. I think the issue is a gap in Certbot’s functionality—specifically, the inability for the user to specify which private key they want to use. As far as I can tell, people are only using CSRs because it’s a way to work around that missing functionality. Therefore, while it would be fine to add a “how to create a CSR” tutorial to Certbot’s documentation, I think it would be even more helpful to more people just to add the private-key feature so that people don’t have to muck around with CSRs in the first place.

> It says that by running letsencrypt-auto I should have gotten dialog boxes asking for my email address and asking me to accept the terms of service. Following your five steps above, I never got those. Also, no /etc/letsencrypt/live directory was created.

The 5 steps I posted above just install the project. You have to actually run it to get dialog boxes or the `/etc/letsencrypt` directory.

> Trying to run letsencrypt-auto after following your five steps above just causes it to hang again.

That's because those directions install the version of the project that was available a couple months ago when I wrote that post. `letsencrypt-auto` automatically updates to the newest version, so when it sees an old version is installed, it tries to update it. The workaround may be like this
1. Add a built function `__wraparmor__`
   It will restore func_code before call function and obfuscate func_code after it returns.

2. Add a decorator `wraparmor`
```
def wraparmor(func):
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        __wraparmor__(func.func_code)
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        __wraparmor__(func.func_code)
        return result
    wrapper.__module__ = func.__module__
    wrapper.__name__ = func.__name__
    wrapper.__doc__ = func.__doc__
    wrapper.__dict__.update(func.__dict__)
    return wrapper
```

3. Compile python scripts to ast node, and add decorator "wraparmor" for each function and class method.

@deepakkumareverest Unfortunately, I don't really have the time right now to help you as much as I would need to. 

First off, Welcome to Python! It's a fun language, but like all new things, it has it's own quirks. You might do well over on https://www.python.org/doc/ or checking for local Python meetups.

Secondly: I'd STRONGLY urge you not to run programs as "root". It's a bit like taking the doors off your house and keeping your savings on the table. While you're learning, you might want to run things in a "safe" account that has permission to only run what it needs to. You can look for tutorials about how to do this by searching for "unix security network ports". This can be a bit tricky as well, so again, if you can find someone who can help teach you, it's a tremendous advantage.

Finally, there are server backends in a variety of languages, including node.js (which is javascript that can be run on the server). If you just want to get things going quickly, and you're more comfortable writing in javascript, that might be a good option as well. With all the new things you'll be learning, it might be nice to remove a few if you can. ;)

Best of luck!

@jenshnielsen I almost copied the Python for loop structure, but I don't instantiate a variable (`i` in your example). Instead, the for loop is `for {some generator}`, the reason being that the variable `i` can't actually be used in a Loop. 

As an explicit example, say we have initialized parameter `p = qc.ManualParameter('test_parameter')`. Writing a loop could then be:

```python
%%measurement test_measurement
for p.sweep(0, 10, step=1):
    p
```

which would translate to
```python
loop = qc.Loop(p.sweep(0, 10, step=1)).each(
    p)
data = loop.get_data_set('test_measurement')
```

For future reference `oauth2client` is depricated in favor of `google-auth`

```python
from datetime import datetime

from requests import exceptions as requests_errors

from google.auth.exceptions import RefreshError
from google.oauth2.credentials import Credentials as GoogleCredentials

from social_django.utils import load_strategy

class Credentials(GoogleCredentials):
    """Google auth credentials using python social auth under the hood"""

    def _parse_expiry(self, data):
        """
        Parses the expiry field from a data into a datetime.

        Args:
             data (Mapping): extra_data from UserSocialAuth model
        Returns:
             datetime: The expiration
        """
        return datetime.fromtimestamp(data['auth_time'] + data['expires'])

    def __init__(self, usa):
        """
        Args:
            usa (UserSocialAuth): UserSocialAuth google-oauth2 object
        """
        backend = usa.get_backend_instance(load_strategy())
        data = usa.extra_data
        token = data['access_token']
        refresh_token = data['refresh_token']
        token_uri = backend.refresh_token_url()
        client_id, client_secret = backend.get_key_and_secret()
        scopes = backend.get_scope()
        # id_token is not provided with GoogleOAuth2 backend
        super().__init__(
            token, refresh_token=refresh_token, id_token=None,
            token_uri=token_uri, client_id=client_id, client_secret=client_secret,
            scopes=scopes
        )
        self.usa = usa
        # Needed for self.expired() check
        self.expiry = self._parse_expiry(data)

    def refresh(self, request):
        """Refreshes the access token.

        Args:
            request (google.auth.transport.Request): The object used to make
                HTTP requests.

        Raises:
            google.auth.exceptions.RefreshError: If the credentials could
                not be refreshed.
        """
        usa = self.usa
        try:
            usa.refresh_token(load_strategy())
        except requests_errors.HTTPError as e:
            raise RefreshError(e)
        data = usa.extra_data
        self.token = data['access_token']
        self._refresh_token = data['refresh_token']
        self.expiry = self._parse_expiry(data)

# Usage
usa = user.social_auth.get(provider="google-oauth2")
service = discovery.build('gmail', 'v1', credentials=Credentials(usa))
emails = service.users().messages().list(
    userId=usa.user.email,
    q='FROM:lane@joinjune.com'
).execute()
```

This is a bit tricky, all of these options seem to require fairly substantial code changes. The option I like the most is fetching functions on demand and getting rid of the import thread. The primary concern I have about that option is that it can make the first function invocation take a while, which can complicate profiling and make things appear slow. This is a big enough change that it'd be good to discuss this in more depth.

Making the C++ local scheduler client thread safe is another potentially good option. Though I would only bother to do that for the Raylet code path and not for the legacy code base (since it requires changes in the backend).

A simpler way to do this might be to give each client two sockets, one that is used to send messages to the backend, and one that is used to read messages from the backend. These sockets could then potentially be protected by different locks. Something like this might work.

If you examine the output of failues, you'll note
 
 - when private, the private_servers array is empty in the source of `https://www.camsoda.com/api/v1/video/vtoken/{0}?username=guest_{1}`
 - meaning no server is defined for connection. Hardcoding this to `private1.camsoda.com` will make no difference. Even assuming that is the correct server address 
 - for streamname, this also is blank. The json attribute is still known as `stream_name` but that isn't parsed correctly when in non-public session
 
It's worth noting that token isn't required for public shows. Ultimately token is just a base64 encoded set of additional attributes.
 
It's highly likely that further connection attributes and/or cookies are now required. Only with network trace or other information can the plugin be fixed, as we're missing information for a valid connection.

All that said, given private (including spy and ticket shows) are subscription elements to the site as in you need to pay to watch these, I see no reason why streamlink should 'fix' this.

#!/bin/bash

function finish {

trap finish EXIT

streamlink ${@:2} &
pid=$!

finish

Mypy is a static analysis tool to check python type annotations, it isn't a
testing tool as such. I just added a test to run it. The type annotations
are specified starting in PEP 484 (
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/). Van Rossum  is actively
involved in mypy (and has even answered some of my trivial questions about
it ...).
The advantages of using mypy, in my opinion, is that it can help make code
more clear by adding type annotations to function signatures and variables,
and checking that these actually make sense (just as in most compiled
languages). The type annotations are ignored at runtime and have no runtime
effects, so you can quickly run python code just as always and ignore mypy
during rapid development, and ideally only worry about typechecking at your
leisure or before code is checked in. I have used this on another project
and have found it to be quite helpful in catching logic errors,
particularly when dealing with checks involving None, but that is just the
most common example. I think this will be a bit more clear if some actual
type annotations are added.

Hi @njsmith and @matham,

Thanks a lot for the detailed discussion. 

Full disclosure, I've never programmed with `asyncio` or `trio` besides some toy examples, so please excuse me if I'm saying something which is clearly wrong or impossible to accomplish. :grin:

I don't know the internal details and consequences of integrating a separate event loop in Trio, so I'm still digesting what has been said here.

---

The use case I had in mind was to allow the user to execute asynchronous code in response to user events, something like:

```python
button.clicked.connect(download_images)

# ...

async def download_images():
    button.setEnabled(False)
    progress_bar = QProgressBar()
    for image_path in paths:
        await download_image(image_path)
        progress_bar.advance()
    button.setEnabled(True)
```

(I understand that's not possible from a technical stand-point, I'm just trying to demonstrate the general idea).

Without getting into the issue of actually integrating into the Qt event loop, I believe we can get the above to work with an approach similar to:

```python
button.clicked.connect(download_images_sync)

def download_images_sync():
    trio.run(download_images)

# ...

async def download_images():
    button.setEnabled(False)
    progress_bar = QProgressBar()
    for image_path in paths:
        await download_image(image_path)
        progress_bar.advance()
    button.setEnabled(True)
```

Of course the problem with this is that `download_images` will block Qt's own event loop, leaving the GUI unresponsive.

[`QApplication.processEvents()`](http://doc.qt.io/qt-5/qcoreapplication.html#processEvents) can be called periodically to avoid blocking the Qt's event loop. What if we schedule two tasks to run at the same time, one doing the original work of downloading the images and another one which just calls `QApplication.processEvents()` periodically?

```python
button.clicked.connect(download_images_sync)

def download_images_sync():
    trio.run(run_with_process_events)

async def run_with_process_events():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        event = trio.Event()
        nursery.start_soon(download_images_monitored, event)
        nursery.start_soon(process_events_tick, event)

async def process_events_tick(event):
    while not event.is_set():
        qApp.processEvents()
        trio.sleep(1 / 20)

async def download_images_monitored(event):
    await download_images()
    event.set()

# ...

async def download_images():
    button.setEnabled(False)
    progress_bar = QProgressBar()
    for image_path in paths:
        await download_image(image_path)
        progress_bar.advance()
    button.setEnabled(True)
```

This looks like it would run `download_images` asynchronously and would keep the GUI responsive. Of course this can eventually be refactored to something nicer to use, for example:

```python
button.clicked.connect(async_binder(download_images))

async def download_images():
    button.setEnabled(False)
    progress_bar = QProgressBar()
    for image_path in paths:
        await download_image(image_path)
        progress_bar.advance()
    button.setEnabled(True)
```

Where `async_binder` would be responsible to create the intermediate management tasks.

But there's the question of what would happen if another user event (another button for example) also wants to execute its asynchronous handler while we are downloading images in `download_images`? What will happen if we call `trio.run` while we are already processing another task?

---

Back to how integrate this into the Qt event loop, what if `trio` exposed a way schedule tasks, and the user could drive task execution themselves? For example (using a fictional API):

```python
task_manager = trio.get_task_manager()

task_manager.schedule(coroutine1)
task_manager.schedule(coroutine2)
# runs whatever task should execute next, until we find an "await" call
task_manager.advance_until_await()  
``` 

IOW, `trio` "event loop" would be something like:

```python
while task_manager.has_running_tasks():
    task_manager.advance_until_await()  
```

If the above is possible, then integrating it into Qt's event loop would be simple:

```python
# normal Qt application:
qApp.exec_loop()

# integrated with trio:
task_manager = trio.get_task_manager()
while some_condition_to_quit_app():
    task_manager.advance_until_await()
    qApp.processEvents()
```

I'm not sure how this would play with trio's internals (specially with I/O), I thought I would throw the idea here and see where it lands.

@belm0 Oh yeah, it's absolutely a real problem, and we need to find a solution one way or another.

The thing about traceback elision that makes me hesitate is, in the long run, we have to find a way to get out of the business of monkeypatching every traceback printer in the world. It's just not maintainable. (See the discussion at the beginning of #611.) And that means that if we want traceback elision to work long-term, we need to come up with a way to move the whole Python ecosystem in that direction. This is doable, and should probably be done: the pytest folks have their own hack for it (`__traceback_hide__`, and since Python switched implementing most of the core import machinery in Python those frames have been causing various problems too (see [bpo-24305](https://bugs.python.org/issue24305) and linked bugs)... but pushing something like this through python-dev is a *ton* of work. Like "plan to spend multiple full-time person-months on it" level of work.

OTOH, keeping the tracebacks simple in the first place is something we can do with relatively small, straightforward changes today. In many cases it's probably simpler than elision (e.g., it's not totally obvious how to write/tune/test the heuristics, and you avoid needing to create/test/document tuning knobs like `MIN_FRAMES_ELIDED`). And, if we run out of low-hanging fruit and the tracebacks are still bad, then when we propose adding elision features to Python we can say we've done our due diligence and proved that the problem really does require interpreter changes.

I guess the way to think about this is that there are really a few separate concepts to disentangle here:

One thing is just freeing resources. This is what `(async) with` is for: you definitely want to do this. But if that were all that was involved then we wouldn't need all this complexity; closing things would just be about freeing memory.

Another is communicating to our peer that we're done (for streams, not so much for files or listeners). The reason `SSLStream` has to send data during `graceful_close` is so that the peer gets a cryptographic record that `graceful_close` was called, which only matters because some protocols assign some semantic meaning to that. I guess file close has a similar issue in that part of why we have to assume `close` is blocking is that we're wrapping potentially-buffered file objects and they have to flush the buffers before closing, and another part is that over NFS the kernel may block while flushing buffers on `close`, and if we had a properly factored stack we could have one version of closing that does the flush and one that doesn't and just abandons things mid-stream...

(I guess there's also multiple levels to this, TLS close_notify is a more elaborate shutdown than sending TCP FIN which is a more elaborate shutdown than the kernel just forgetting about the socket entirely. Not that we would want to do that, sending FIN is good, but it illustrates that there isn't a fundamental theoretical cleanup/no-cleanup distinction here, it's more context-dependent and pragmatic than that.)

I suppose the connection is that sending FIN is useful for telling the other side that they can free memory. Plus obviously it's handy that the kernel is willing to take over best-effort sending and re-sending of FIN.

In general, my preference is to avoid magic introspection, dispatch, subclassing, and similar things... I like delegation and explicit lists :-). In theory, it's extra typing and they can get out-of-sync with Python, but in practice I think it's worth it because even if it does go wrong sometimes, it's much easier to fix than complicated magic. Example: in trio's socket wrapper, here are the attributes that [get delegated directly](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/5d046ba7613c2a3063c3fc93fc4b9a9d94cdd8b0/trio/socket.py#L235-L246) and [here's one that gets wrapped](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/5d046ba7613c2a3063c3fc93fc4b9a9d94cdd8b0/trio/socket.py#L527-L528).

I guess in this case the problem is that the `io` module is a messy tangle of subclasses? But even so, it seems like it should be pretty straightforward to keep a list of which classes we want to wrap (basically `Path`, `TextIO`, `BufferedReader`, `BufferedWriter`, `BufferedRandom`?), and which methods are sync and which need to be wrapped in `run_in_worker_thread`. Hi,

I noticed a lot of code is reused from the DQN agent and the Dfdq agent implementations which will affect future maintainability of the code. If you could wrap the code that should be shared between the two implementations in methods, it would improve readability. 

Another thing I noticed was that it seems like the pretraining phase will run steps in the environment (among other things being that run during normal training phases) which seems unnecessary considering these steps aren't recorded into the memory. Avoiding this can substantially increase performance in some use cases.  My recommendation here would be to decouple this part from the rest of the implementation by wrapping the training of the network in its own method. This would also improve code readability and maintability as mentioned above. 

I haven't gone through all the code line by line but if I find the time I will try and verify that everything in your pull requests works as aspect. 

Great job so far though, I'm finding good use of the additions you've made in a personal project of mine.

I've been slow to get around to this but I have implemented one solution to this feature request in #21 . First, I changed up how the slicing filters produce an output on the backend (using composite datasets rather than appended unstructured grids) to allow us to have data structures that could be saved out separately. Then I added a new filter `PVGeo.filters.ExtractCellCenters` (ParaView's native Cell Centers filter wasn't working for this) that will create XYZ+atts data of the cell centers for any input dataset's cell data. If you use this filter on your sliced dataset (e.g. from `PVGeo.filters.ManySlicesAlongAxis`) then you can use ParaView's native CSV writer to save out the XYZ+atts data for each slice individually for use in Surfer or other programs while preserving resolution somewhat well. It's important to keep track of what attributes you are looking at in ParaView (cells vs. points). If your sliced dataset only contains PointData then just go ahead and save the slices use ParaView's CSV writer. However, if you would like to save the cell data (what we use in the UBC grids) then you must follow the process above which I demonstrate in [this lengthy video](https://vimeo.com/286429857). To tell apart Cell data from Point data, ParaView uses a convenient graphic when selecting display attribute where a point looking diamond thing denotes points and a cube/voxel represents cell data like shown in the following screen capture:
https://review.gerrithub.io/#/c/citellusorg/citellus/+/411478
